taskDescription: Click on the key vertebral column anatomy and clinical terms in the text below.
textField:
The *vertebral* column has four *curvatures*: cervical *lordosis*, thoracic *kyphosis*, lumbar lordosis, and sacral kyphosis. *Primary* curvatures (thoracic and sacral) are present at *birth*. *Secondary* curvatures (cervical and lumbar) develop *postnatally* with head control and walking. There are *33* vertebrae: 7 *cervical*, 12 *thoracic*, 5 *lumbar*, 5 fused *sacral*, and 4 fused *coccygeal*.

The *intervertebral* disc has a central *nucleus* *pulposus* (a remnant of the *notochord*) surrounded by the *annulus* *fibrosus*. *Disc* *prolapse* most commonly affects the *L4-L5* and L5-S1 levels, accounting for 90% of cases. *Lumbar* *puncture* is performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5 because the *spinal* cord ends at L1-L2. The *ligamentum* *flavum* produces a characteristic pop during the procedure.

*Spondylolisthesis* involves anterior slipping of one vertebra, most commonly L4 over L5. *Tuberculosis* of the spine (*Pott's* disease) is the most common cause of *spinal* cord *compression* in India. *Ankylosing* *spondylitis* affects the *sacroiliac* joint and is associated with *HLA-B27*. Cervical vertebrae have *transverse* *foramina* for the *vertebral* arteries.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Revisit the terms you missed.
Excellent! You identified the key vertebral column terms accurately.
